title: Spring Parasites and Pests: Protecting Your Pet in Portland
description: Learn how to protect your dog or cat from ticks, fleas, and other spring parasites common in the Portland, Oregon area. Prevention tips from our NW Portland veterinarians.
author: Dr. Lyn Bedsaul, DVM, CCRP
content:
Spring in Portland means warmer weather, blooming trails, and unfortunately, a surge in parasites that can affect your dog or cat. At Forest Heights Veterinary Clinic, we see a sharp increase in flea and tick cases starting in March and peaking through June.

## Ticks in the Portland Area

Oregon is home to several tick species, including the Western black-legged tick, which can carry Lyme disease. Portland's proximity to Forest Park and the West Hills means many of our patients spend time in prime tick habitat. After hikes or off-leash play in wooded areas, check your pet thoroughly — especially around the ears, between toes, and under the collar.

If you find a tick, use fine-tipped tweezers to grasp it as close to the skin as possible and pull straight out with steady pressure. Save the tick in a sealed bag and bring it to your next appointment so we can identify the species.

## Fleas: Not Just a Summer Problem

In the Pacific Northwest, fleas can be active year-round thanks to our mild winters, but infestations explode in spring. A single flea can lay up to 50 eggs per day, and those eggs fall into your carpet, bedding, and upholstery. By the time you see fleas on your pet, your home may already have thousands of eggs developing.

Signs of flea infestation include excessive scratching, small dark specks (flea dirt) in your pet's fur, hair loss around the tail base, and red, irritated skin. Some pets develop flea allergy dermatitis, an intense allergic reaction to flea saliva that causes severe itching from even a single bite.

## Intestinal Parasites

Spring rains create the perfect conditions for intestinal parasites like roundworms, hookworms, and giardia. Puddles, muddy trails, and dog parks are common sources of exposure. We recommend a fecal exam at your pet's annual wellness visit to screen for these parasites, many of which can be transmitted to humans.

## Prevention Is Key

Year-round parasite prevention is the most effective approach. We carry a range of flea, tick, and heartworm preventives and can recommend the right product for your pet's lifestyle. If your pet spends time outdoors in Forest Park, the West Hills, or neighborhood trails around Forest Heights and West Slope, consistent prevention is especially important.

Call us at (503) 291-1757 to discuss the best parasite prevention plan for your pet, or ask about it at your next wellness exam.
